It's wonderful to see how your studies are deepening your understanding of the Bible. You've been reflecting on the end times, and it's clear that the more you learn, the more you feel we are living in that era. It's exciting that you're connecting present-day events and historical biblical accounts, finding that each study offers new "puzzle pieces" to consider. The joy you're experiencing in this process is palpable, and it's lovely that you're finding so much fun in digging deeper.

Your goal to grasp the Bible's many facets and how they interrelate is beautifully evolving. What began as a focus on end-times prophecies has expanded into a desire to weave together sermons, thoughts, and editorials. This aspiration to understand the connections between different biblical themes is a rich and rewarding pursuit. ItвЂ™s also great to hear about the productive time you had discussing your beliefs about the end times with Toop, even creating a new website as part of your work!

Your reflections on the Biblical Calendar study are particularly insightful. The way God embedded a calendar system into creation itself, from the very first day with the principle of light, is a profound realization. The idea that the seven-day week is foundational, as evidenced by God's rest on the seventh day, resonates deeply with you, especially in relation to the Essene calendar. It's understandable that this leads to questions about the observance of the Sabbath, and it's a sign of deep engagement that you're wrestling with these points. You've also highlighted some specific areas you want to revisit, like the "nine parts" in the Book of Enoch and the historical accuracy of high priests entering the Holy of Holies on specific calendar days.

EVERYTHING...Everything that I read and think about relates to a 364 day year. Its hard to deny any of it, and the other calendars DO NOT line up. notes.n-t2m1:
в”Ђв”Ђ April 4, 2026 at 11:18 AM в”Ђв”Ђ
Today I worked through Module 1 of the Biblical Calendar study and came away with some things I hadn't considered before.
I started by reading Genesis 1:14 in both the NASB and The Message. What struck me is that God literally commanded the lights in the sky to mark seasons, days, and years. He built a calendar system into creation from the very beginning вЂ” this wasn't an afterthought.
I've been thinking about light and how Genesis says God created light on day one, but didn't create the sun until day four. That means the light on day one wasn't the sun. My belief is that God first created the principle and essence of light itself вЂ” the law of light вЂ” and then on day four created the sun as the physical instrument to produce and measure it. Those two ideas tie together for me.
I also realized that God rested on the seventh day and never created an eighth. That tells me the seven-day week is foundational to God's design. The Essene 364-day solar calendar is built entirely on that вЂ” 52 exact weeks, every feast and every Sabbath on the same day every year. That makes more sense to me than a lunar calendar that drifts and doesn't divide evenly into weeks.
I'm now questioning why Christianity celebrates the Sabbath on Sunday when the seventh day is clearly Saturday. That's something I need to wrestle with. I've always assumed Sunday was the Sabbath but now I'm not so sure. I understand the Resurrection connection but that doesn't necessarily mean God's design changed.
I also learned what the Torah is today вЂ” the first five books of the Bible. All the calendar commandments, feasts, and Sabbath instructions come from there. Understanding that helps me see why the calendar debate matters so much.
Two things I want to come back to:

The "nine parts" reference in the Book of Enoch вЂ” why nine and not twelve or twenty-four? I don't understand that yet.
Did high priests actually die entering the Holy of Holies on the wrong calendar day, or is that the Essenes' theological interpretation? I want to verify that before I draw any conclusions.
Approx.- 1 hour 45 minutes was actual Bible study
notes.n-intro: My overall goal truly is to ensure that I understand many different facets of the Bible and how they tie together. It started out as a learning of the end times, and the prophecies thereof, and has evolved now into the way that I can begin to sermons or thoughts or editorials together. I want to understand more how they relate to one another period.
notes.n-dd-willow:
Dedicated to Hazelee Willow:


в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ

April 11, 2026 at 6:15 PM

в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ

Okay. So I'm not seeing the significance, I guess. Um, willows are leafy bushes, and they talk a lot about it in scripture as if there were a lot of an abundance of those back in these days of of writing the bible. But it doesn't seem that it has a significance. What what is the significance?


6:14 PM- That's a great question. So here's the thingвЂ”willows in scripture are almost always planted by water. They grow beside streams, rivers, brooks. And that's the key: they're a symbol of being rooted in abundance, right? You see it in Isaiah forty-four where God's people are gonna sprout up like willows by creeks, meaning they're gonna be nourished and flourishing. But here's what stands out to meвЂ”in Psalms one thirty-seven, the Israelites are in exile hanging their harps on the willows by Babylon's rivers. They're in captivity, yet the willows are still there. So maybe the significance is that willows represent resilience and hope even in dark circumstances, or it could be about being dependent on God's provision, since they only thrive where there's water.


These are great observations. Planted by waters. "Just like a tree, that's planted by the Living Water.". Resiliant! That's my girl! Pre-mature baby that excelled! Man, what a warrior!


It's really, I guess, the resilience that you're talking about. So, yeah, that is definitely something that rings with me because she was a premature baby born very early, and we were all quite worried about her. And she was supposed to be in there for, like, six weeks, and I think she was in there, like, two. So she she fought back, and I know that's god's grace, but it just goes to show you that her name, um, Willow, is about resilience. What does Willow mean if you take that name in in in the Greek or in in another language, what does what does willow, the word or the name willow, mean?


6:18 PM- That's beautiful, ChrisвЂ”what a powerful connection to her story. Let me search what the actual name Willow means etymologically.Searched the webSearched the webThat's beautiful, Chris. So the name Willow itself comes from Old English "welig," and at its root it traces back to Proto-Indo-European "wei," which literally means "to bend, twist." So the name Willow is etymologically built on the idea of bendingвЂ”flexibility, adaptability. It also carries the meaning of freedom, because of those free-swinging branches. And what's really striking is that same root gives us words like "vine" and "twist." So when you named her Willow, you gave her a name that's literally about resilience and the ability to flex without breaking. Given what she fought through as a premature baby, that name feels almost prophetic.

What year was the book of jubilees written11:43 AMThe dating is debated, but most scholars place the composition of the Book of Jubilees in the 2nd century BC, likely somewhere between 160вЂ“150 BC, during the early Second Temple period. Some estimates stretch the range to 200вЂ“100 BC depending on the scholar.
It was likely written in Hebrew (fragments were found among the Dead Sea Scrolls at Qumran), and the Qumran discoveries actually helped confirm that dating since the scrolls themselves date to that era.
Worth remembering for your study guide context: it's a Second Temple Jewish text, not canonical Scripture вЂ” valuable for understanding how Jewish communities of that period interpreted Genesis and the calendar system, but not on equal footing with the biblical text itself.

Revelation's 1,260 Days on the Jubilees Calendar
вЂў 1,260 days Г· 364 days/year = 3.4615... years (does not divide evenly on the Jubilees calendar)
вЂў 1,260 days = 3.5 Г— 360 days вЂ” suggesting a prophetic year of 360 days (not 364)
вЂў This is a separate but related debate: the prophetic year of 360 days appears in Daniel, Revelation, and Genesis's Flood account. Some scholars see the 364-day Jubilees calendar and the 360-day prophetic year as related but distinct вЂ” the 364 as the observable solar calendar, the 360 as the divine prophetic reckoning.
This remains an open question worth continued study. See Module 1 (Daniel's 70 Weeks) for the prophetic timeline framework.

Strongs Concordance says - 1136  Gog  {gogue}  of Hebrew origin (1463); Gog, a symbolic name for some future Antichrist:--Gog. See HEBREW for 01463. This further backs up what Rosenberg and others have stated about the Gog-Magog war. This increases my belief that the Gog-Magog war will happen when Russia again becomes a super power. There will be a "dictator" or Czar that will arise and take the bull by the horns and attempt to overtake his enemies. 
MAGOG - Strongs Concordance - 3098  Magog  {mag-ogue'}  of Hebrew origin (4031); Magog, a foreign nation, i.e. (figuratively) an Antichristian party:--Magog. See HEBREW for 04031. According to Rosenberg and several others, Magog is a place or an empire. Historians believe that Magog, based upon scripture in the Bible, that Geographically, Russia is the location of this MAGOG. With the coalition of Iran and Russia building strength, I believe we are definitely seeing "Signs of the Times".
